summary: Atwood revisits the legendary 'Black Sunday' hack from 2001, where DirecTV executed a masterful counter-attack against smart card pirates who had been stealing satellite TV signals for years. The hackers had reverse-engineered DirecTV's 'H' cards and created an ecosystem of tools to bypass subscription controls. DirecTV's brilliant response involved sending seemingly useless code updates over two months that assembled into a devastating weapon, destroying an estimated 100,000 hacked cards one week before the Super Bowl. The attack was 'signed' by rewriting the first 8 bytes of hacked cards to read 'GAME OVER.' A 2008 Wired article finally revealed Christopher Tarnovsky as the mastermind behind this elaborate counter-hack.
key_points: DirecTV's H cards had design flaws that allowed hackers to reverse-engineer them and receive all channels for free, with no way for DirecTV to track abuse since users could unplug phone lines
The hacking community had defeated every DirecTV countermeasure for nearly two years, with hacked H cards selling for over $400 on eBay
DirecTV's winning strategy was sending attack code in ~60 parts over two months, disguised as useless updates, that assembled into a program capable of destroying pirate cards
The Black Sunday kill destroyed approximately 100,000 hacked cards in one evening, eliminating 98% of the hacking community's capability
Christopher Tarnovsky was later revealed as the architect of this sophisticated counter-attack
money_quotes: The first 8 computer bytes of all hacked cards were rewritten to read 'GAME OVER.'
Like a final piece of a puzzle allowing the entire picture, the final updates made all the useless bits of computer code join into a dynamic program, existing on the card itself.
The Black Sunday attack code was sent to pirate cards in about five dozen parts over the course of two months, like a tank transported piece by piece to a battlefield to be assembled in the field.
In a masterful, planned, and orchestrated manner, DirecTV had updated the old and ailing technology.
The IRC DirecTV channels overflowed with thousands of people who had lost the ability to watch their stolen TV.
For the first time, they could see a cunning mind at work on the other side.
It's a gripping tale of high stakes programming, a life of electronic warfare with millions of dollars at risk on both sides.
key_insight: The most elegant hacks aren't brute force attacks but patient, strategic operations that hide in plain sight until the moment of execution.
